result: I keep coming back to the sound of wind in poplar leaves, the way it turns light into currency, spending itself across the yard in small, reckless flashes. Yesterday I found a cricket sleeping inside my shoe, its body a trembling comma against the leather, as if the sentence of summer still needed pausing. I left it there; some things deserve to finish their thoughts uninterrupted. My mother used to say the world is stitched together by minor mercies—too small to list on tax forms or gravestones, yet they hold the seams when grief pulls. I didn’t believe her until the year the river took the bridge and the town still shared coffee, passing dented pots from porch to porch across the muddy gap, measuring survival in scoops of grounds. Now I collect those mercies like beach glass, pocketing the pale edges: the way the neighbor kid pronounces “ambulance” with an extra syllable, how the library never fines the homeless man for overdue Westerns, the hush that falls over the laundromat when someone folds a stranger’s forgotten dress shirt and sets it on the drier like an offering. None of it solves the nightly news, but it keeps the sky from unscrewing itself and falling. Tonight I’ll set the crickets free, open both doors, let the house breathe whatever futures it can catch. Somewhere a poplar is spending its last coin of light; I want to stand beneath it, palms up, wallet empty, rich enough to receive.

My mother used to say the world is stitched together by minor mercies—too small to list on tax forms or gravestones, yet they hold the seams when grief pulls. I didn’t believe her until the year the river took the bridge and the town still shared coffee, passing dented pots from porch to porch across the muddy gap, measuring survival in scoops of grounds. Now I collect those mercies like beach glass, pocketing the pale edges: the way the neighbor kid pronounces “ambulance” with an extra syllable, how the library never fines the homeless man for overdue Westerns, the hush that falls over the laundromat when someone folds a stranger’s forgotten dress shirt and sets it on the drier like an offering. None of it solves the nightly news, but it keeps the sky from unscrewing itself and falling. Tonight I’ll set the crickets free, open both doors, let the house breathe whatever futures it can catch.
